title: What does it do…. Absolutely nothing
body: This was such a disappointment. I put 2 apples out and filled it with the solution that was given and you can tell it’s vinegar. Vinegar is a very strong scent as it is and I had 2 small apples. And even though they were small I could still smell it when I am in my room.. I placed one by my kitchen trash can and one close to my kitchen sink. And it attracted a lot of these fruit flies. BUT that’s all it did and they were all just posted on top of the apple. If I were to move my hand over the apple they just all fly away at the same time.. I’m just wondering what it does. Do they go inside do they die. So far it just looks like it just attracts them to the top of the apple. Nothing else. If I were to try and throw the apple away I’m just going to have a face full of fruit flies.. these things are super annoying. So if you’re looking to kill them or get rid of them do not buy this item.. if you’re looking to collect them 🤷🏻‍♀️ then this would be a great buy. Idk for me I am not satisfied because I am definitely not trying to collect them.
date: Reviewed in the United States on March 2, 2026
author: Cali Love

title: Do not buy, make's the problem worse.
body: I bought these along with the other brand to see if they worked better than the homemade traps, they do very good catching them but the problem is the holes are way too big. My problem got worse as they continued to breed and I could not find out how I poured boiling hot water down my drains everything was clean until last night I noticed upstair's in the bathroom they were going in and out of the trap especially the large center hole and there was larvae in it. I threw all traps away and am using my diy traps and the issue is already getting better. Search diy fruit fly traps and you will have much better luck I like to rinse out and drill small holes and I mean small not like this companies in 2 liter bottles along the top sides and the cap and put apple cider vinegar with a drop of dish soap and some sugar in it if you have some fermented juice they love it too basically anything fermented you can add to the mix. It also helps to wrap or stick sticky traps around the traps to catch even more. Be aware they breed in house plant soil (how I got mine but I've sense taken them outside), drains, trash cans and keep everything clean you can also pour bleach down drains. This company is just trying to get you to keep buying these things because you can't reuse them they just keep breeding and you just keep buying more it's a never ending cycle even though your killing them they just multiply. I can't speak for the other company that sells a similar product but I think the same thing happens with that.
date: Reviewed in the United States on July 9, 2025
author: I_Am_Iron_Man

title: Liquid Lure does not attract fruit flies - worthless
body: The cute little red plastic apples are a great design. It’s too bad they are paired with lure which does not work.I’ve placed four of the six apples in areas of breeding and concentration. One on the table with plants, one right in front of me on my desk where I work, one by the kitchen sink, and one on the kitchen island next to another plant.They have been out for a couple weeks now. And not a single fruit fly can be seen inside any of the traps! In fact, I’ve already killed two flying round me this morning with the apple trap less than two feet away. Yesterday I found one fruit fly crawling around the window sill in the kitchen mere inches away from the trap. It never did go in the trap!I don’t know if the lure expires or not. It does have a faint vinegary smell. But as these were sold to me, they are absolutely worthless!

author: Agrarian

title: Stinky and Ineffective
body: Absolutely. Turning the sarcasm dial to *unreasonable*:---I am currently living in an active infestation. I have no idea where these creatures came from or how they managed to establish a thriving civilization so quickly, having deep-cleaned my room and offering absolutely no food, water, or emotional support to these bugs. And yet, they have moved in and declared sovereignty.In a panic, I bought several traps. This one now lives on my work desk, because nothing says “professional” like taking meetings while insects launch themselves into your face like kamikaze pilots.The trap smells VILE, like rotting fruit and decay. I can smell it the moment I enter the room. The bugs are clearly obsessed with the scent, but instead of going inside the trap like advertised, they just loiter around it like it’s a sketchy bar.After more than 24 hours, one trap caught **five** bugs. The other caught **none**. Meanwhile, a different brand trap elsewhere has racked up a body count, which confirms this product’s only real function is making my room smell awful.Would not buy again unless I decide my room needs to smell like death **and** still have bugs.
date: Reviewed in the United States on December 14, 2025
author: Ari

title: Does not attract or trap fruit flies--dish soap or plug-in light works much better
body: DOES NOT WORK. I have a gnat infestation in my indoor office plants. Because they are in my office, where I spend 8 hours per day, I needed them gone, stat. I tried 3 different methods of gnat elimination: (1) Terro apple-shaped fly traps; (2) Shallow dishes of liquid dish soap; (3) Zevo plug-in flying insect trap.After 3 days, here are the results:(1) Terro apple-shaped fly traps: Easy to set up and fill with their "special" gnat-attracting formula which I am sure is just apple cider vinegar. The apple containers are small and cute. But, they don't work. I CAUGHT 1 (ONE) GNAT using 4 different traps placed on top of soil in my plant containers.(2) Shallow dishes of dawn dish soap: Easy to set up and fill. However, the liquid soap starts to dry out and harden after several days. They are a bit of a pain to wash out and refill, icks me out, plus I think it's a waste of dish soap. I CAUGHT 50 GNATS using this method--I used 4 different shallow containers placed on top of soil in my plant containers.(3) Zevo plug-in flying insect trap. This plug-in fly trap was super easy to set up. It's basically a blue light that attracts the insects and super sticky paper (comes with replacement inserts) to trap them. I CAUGHT 40 GNATS using this method. This trap is the furthest from my plants--it's about 6 feet away. If I move it closer, it will probably work even better. This is my favorite scent-free, mess-free method that actually works.If I didn't use the other 2 methods, I would have about 100 more gnats still flying around my office space, ick. Save your money and don't buy.
date: Reviewed in the United States on June 4, 2024

title: Does not work at all

We keep everything in the store spotless, but we'll still get a couple of those little visitors from time to time. I placed 2 traps and after a week, zero got caught. I then added the other 2 traps that came in the package. It has been a few more days since that, and still none has fallen into the trap. They liquid in them smells 100% like vinegar so that is the predominant smell in the room / area where the traps are placed. Looks like I'll be trying a different product because as I said, this one did not work for us at all.

NOT ONE GNAT in the traps. One gnat ON the trap but he was like yeah, I'm good out here!Since I can't return the product now that I have used them, I am out some beans. author: Dorothy Lynch

title: Eliminating fruit flies by making more of them?
body: This is a terrible product. I bought 4 of these and then my wife bought another 4 unbeknownst to me. Will these kill fruit flies? Absolutely. So why the 1 star review? They'll make more fruit flies than they'll kill. They don't kill off all of the fruit flies. The ones that survive reproduce and make even more fruit flies. We went from having a dozen to dozens of dozens. Our kitchen is crawling with them. The traps allow the fruit flies to move in and out freely so the flies that don't die just reproduce more resistant flies.How did I end up fixing this Terro created problem? By making my own fruit fly traps with jars filled with red wine, plastic wrap over the top with a rubber band to hold it in place, and then poking holes through them. Cost me nothing since I had these objects already on hand. I will never buy this product again.

The attractant smells like vinegar and you can squirt it thru the top hole; however not sure how you can pry the top off to clean it. Previously I have used the FlyFix Fruit fly trap. It is bigger and the top screws off for cleaning and filling. I have always used apple cider vinegar with excellent results. They are a little more expensive but worth it if you have fruit fly problems. No need to keep buying attractant as apple cider vinegar works well; some people put slices of banana in the trap and claim it works well. I’m going to try it! I just ordered some more FlyFix fruit fly traps.

title: Cute, convenient containers. But it's just vinegar and dishwashing detergent that didn't work alone.
body: Love the cute, convenient containers. But it's literally just vinegar and dishwashing detergent that didn't work alone, and that's what I had tried on my own without much success before buying 4 of these traps for $14. After an entire day of catching NO fruit flies with these, I looked at some YouTube videos and saw a guy who tried BALSAMIC vinegar instead of white vinegar or apple cider vinegar (which is what I had used.) I added the Balsamic vinegar to the Terro solution and left the house to run some errands. When I returned just an hour later, one of the traps already had 10 flies in it. By last night (24 hours later) there were almost 20 in one of them and 10 in another. I'm hoping that by tonight our bizarre infestation will be almost eradicated. But it's the BALSAMIC VINEGAR that I added that made it effective. I don't regret my purchase because these containers were worth the initial investment. My brother said that these used to have a pheromone in them to attract fruit flies but apparently from the reviews, they changed the formula over the past couple of years, and there was nothing in the ingredient list besides the detergent and vinegar.

author: Songbird-NOLA

title: Greenway traps are better than Terro
body: Greenway traps each caught a dozen fruit flies in a couple of days. The only downside is that they don't catch other species of small bugs. (For those pour bleach down the drain.) My Terro traps caught only a few before the liquid evaporated. The Greenway bottle design keeps them from spilling, and also from evaporating. Terro traps have several big holes so they can spill, and that also allows the liquid to evaporate faster. Terro comes with refills but Greenway doesn't need them. The bugs can also get out of the Terro as easily as getting in, but the Greenway traps hold them until the fumes kill them. They can't climb out with the funnel opening for the same reason it doesn't spill.

title: It works but… needlessly expensive
body: We had a sudden fruit fly infestation. I’m familiar with TERRO ant traps so I had no hesitation ordering. The amount of solution is insufficient.  I chose to empty the traps out every few days, but only had enough solution for 2 uses. Almost $13 for a solution you can make for less than 25 cents. I reused the apple containers but any small plastic container will do. Took about 2-3 weeks to eradicate them. Watch YouTube for instructions, save yourself a lot of money.  Apple cider vinegar, 1 drop dish detergent & your set.  I also used baking soda & plain vinegar in each drain at night, allow it to foam up, close the drains, fill sink with about an inch of water.  Another tip is if you have a garbage disposal try to keep that drain closed as much as possible when not in use.  PS - I  also  tried Green Gobbler it did absolutely nothing.

I saw these and thought I'd give them a try since they cost less, don't use a wall socket, and had great reviews. I put one near my counter top small compost bin and one near the bananas. I placed 2 others near where I have seen the gnats or  fruit flies. The only one that caught anything was the one near the bananas. The others have no more than one dead fruit fly and one has none. So, not buying these again. I'll stick with the sticky pads with the blue light.

title: Works but not well and does not last 90 days
body: While I like this product. I will say that it only slightly works.I have had the product in use for 10 days. In that 10 days it was not uncommen to watch a fruit fly land on the container and run around, going in and out, then fly off.  I would say maybe half of the fruit flys went into the liquid.  I also used a small plastic container with water, apple cider vinegar and liquid dish soap and had more fruit flys in that liquid.As for the “last 90 days” this is completly false. I put out 2 containers and used one bottle with half of the bottle in each container as per instructions. In 9 days the liquid completly dried out.Basically do not waster your money. Just take a small plastic container, low height, add water apple cider vinegar and a couple drops of dish soap. Mix it around. This works better but is not as nice looking.

title: Fruit Fly Trap
body: There are too many holes, and they are much too large. The fruit flies easily go in and out of the trap. The liquid evaporates too fast to last 30 days. The liquid was gone in just 2 weeks and only caught about 4 bugs in that amount of time.I then used my grandmother's version of a fruit fly trap using a small relish jar with 2 tiny holes drilled in the lid. The solution is apple cider vinegar. In just the 2weeks the Terror trap was in place, the homemade trap caught 2 dozen fruit flies & the liquid hasn't evaporated. In 30 days, the whole surface of the liquid in the glass jar was covered in fruit flies.

author: Rita Nadeau

title: Not Very Effective
body: Frankly unimpressed. Before getting these, I used a bowl with a TBS of sugar, half a cup of warm water and a few tablespoons of apple cider vinegar with a few drops of Dawn. I also have three Zevo insect traps (basically very sticky plastic inside a plug in that has a warm blue light). The bowl traps were far more effective at getting a dozen of the buggers trapped (fruit fly invasion, and I do mean invasion). The "bait" in these apple traps obviously contains apple cider vinegar. But the flys would crawl around on the outside and only a few would actually end up in the trap. These traps are very small, and supposedly last 45 days which is ridiculous. After only a few days, they stopped working completely. The Zevo traps, on the other hand, had well over a hundred flies trapped. I had to change those traps out twice a week (it's normally like a month or two before they have to be changed, but INVASION of fruit flies). They are much more effective.

Yes, some what, but not as effective as I hoped.Here’s a better solution: Pour a couple of ounces of apple cider vinegar into a bottom-flat container (I use an old glass jam jar) and add a bit of dish soap. This works WAY better than the TERRO Fruit Fly Trap. How much better? author: kzar

title: Mostly effective, and for German roach nymphs
body: These have been somewhat effective. While I have seen less of these pests when I've got this active, I've also seen them literally just hangout on top of the apple. It's like it can see its dead incestuous disgusting family in the goo and it actually takes it as a warning to not proceed. Perhaps I should stop reusing the same trap...Once this stuff runs out, the pests start right back up again. Not as dense as before, but it is noticeable. I'd rather have this than nothing, that's for sure.I wish this came with way more of the liquid. I feel like I have too many apples and not enough liquid. This seller doesn't appear to sell the liquid in bulk, you just have to buy more traps, which is annoying.I also prefer filling it way more than it suggests because if I fill it less, then I have to replace it more frequently. Ergo, providing more of the liquid would be far more useful.This does emit a faint smell, but I've found that it only smells when I'm directly above it.Make sure to tell your guests or family that this thing can spill if you pick it up wrong.I'd ideally rate this maybe 3.5 stars. It works well enough to keep the pests down, but it's not a permanent solution and they don't always fall for the trap.Update: I've changed my mind. Solid 4 stars. I noticed German roach nymphs wading dead in the liquid. I've been keeping these near my sink because that's where the fruit flies hang out. It appears as though the roaches, while trying to get a drink in the sink, find the fruit fly trap and want in. I have a small infestation of roaches in my place, so if they don't fall for one trap, I'm glad they'll fall for another. I don't like having roach traps on my counter due to the chemicals used, so this much-less-poisonous-to-humans trap is a great alternative.

title: Worked on fungus gnats with other steps in review
body: They work but you must take & do extra steps. We were dealing with a fungus gnat issue. Along with these apples, I put a drop or two of dawn dish soap in them. I also, put 1/2 cup each of baking soda, then salt, & then one cup white vinegar down every drain in the house & left sit til morning & boiled water to pour over each drain. I wiped dry all sinks & tub drains & covered them with stoppers. Every 3 days I would just pour plain boiling water down drain & dry area & keep all drains covered/closed. They are cute bright, tiny , plastic containers with zero noise but captured a lot of these darn fungus gnats. I just refilled them with white vinegar & few drops of dawn dish soap as I treat the drains. All fungus gnats are gone by following the steps above.

author: @Peachtowndivacat007

title: No magic here, but it does sorta work
body: Lately, we've been buying more fruit than usual.  Something we brought home must have had a few fruit flies or eggs, so we've got some of the pesky critters flying around in the kitchen.  It's not a big infestation, but I do see maybe a half dozen of them a day.  So, I've tried a couple of things.I made a couple of do-it-yourself traps.  There are a couple of them on the internet.  It's apple cider vinegar in a mason jar with small holes in the lid (or a cup with plastic wrap with holes).  I made both, and caught a few flies.Then I bought the Terro traps.  They are cute and inexpensive.  I'm pretty sure the magic "attractant" is apple cider vinegar with some kind of surfactant to make sure the critters don't float when they hit the liquid.  I'm guessing a drop of dish wash detergent would work for that purpose.  These also caught a few flies. On several occasions, I've seen them land on the lid and walk around for a while and then fly away. I don't know why they don't go inside the holes - one of life's great mysteries.Then I bought a trap with blue light and sticky paper.  This also caught a few.The most effective (and most fun) is to use our Dyson stick vacuum.  We have white cabinets, so it's easy to see the files when they land.  I've observed that they tend to like vertical surfaces and generally orient themselves head-up.  They also take off backwards.  So, if you get the vacuum running on high and sneak up on them from below, you can generally suck them in. At least for me, this has some entertainment value.  My wife tells me that I remind her of Bill Murray in Ghostbusters with that thing. But it definitely does work.  Who you gonna call...?Bottom line...  I haven't found any magic solution to the problem. Everything seems to work about equally well.  I have my homemade traps, the Terro traps and the light trap all deployed.  There is some value in purchasing the Terro traps, because they are the most attractive option.  They are also inexpensive.  They look like little apples.  Don't get hung up on their "attractant".  Apple cider vinegar and dishwasher detergent should be an effective substitute.Happy hunting!
date: Reviewed in the United States on September 28, 2023
author: Robert A. Sacks

title: Best fruit fly trap I have found
body: Fruit flies are an annoyance and something that we have regularly in our household because we have guinea pigs that seem to attract them.  These traps do a great job with attracting the fruit flies and capturing them, I am always amazed at how many end up in there and it does seem to cut down on the population quite a bit.  I marked it down one star just because it is a bit pricey for the number of traps I feel, and the efficacy seems to drop off significantly after the first few days, but for those days where they are at their peak they really do the job.  I will buy them again (in fact, I put them on subscribe and save because I know I will need them.  They are still the best fruit fly trap we have found.

author: Melba Harmon

title: These do attract them. Read my method
body: These do catch them but won't solve your issue. Definitely good to have on hand.I recently had a gnat or fruit fly issue and felt disgusting and became crazier than a bed bug cleaning like a psycho to my already clean home.  I spent 100 bucks on everything in 3 days. I am surprised I didn't end up in the ER with all the chemicals I used. The vinegar, dishsoap, homemade traps do not work!!This is what works!!!! Get some drain opener. These effers come from the drain and we had a ton of rain. Use it. Clean your entire house with bleach. Put sugar water, soda or anything sweet in an old pan you don't care about. Put in the oven and leave your oven open overnight. Wake up. Shut the oven. Bake those effers at 450 for 10 min. Gone. Also get the gross fly strips AND THESE to catch the stragglers. Put bleach in every drain to sit at every chance. Boil water on your stove and dump it with bleach down your garbage disposal and run it. Have not seen one since. Don't use a garbage disposal. They are just gross sorry. Dichotomus earth/bedbug powder/silica powder under your sinks (kills any bugs exoskeleton). You're welcome.

title: TRICK to make liquid lure last longer READ
body: Does it work ? Yes, but takes a few days to start catching them.Does it catch them all ? AlmostThe liquid lure made them come a running, but took them a few hours to go in the holes of the trap.Trick below to make the liquid lure provided with this product last longer blow.Pop the trap containers lid off that's provided with this kit, dump some honey 🍯 in first, but only enough to cover the bottom of the container floor.Then snap the traps lid cover back on, now use the lure solution provided, but just squeeze enough out to just cover the top of the honey, doesn't need to be deep.Bingo ready...Making more traps .I also took a woman's shaving gel cap lid and made more traps.The shaving cap lid did just as well as the small catch containers provided with this product.Simply I was able to make more traps and make the lure solution provided in the kit last way way longer saving $$$$.Note, I did try to make the home version of this trap with apple cider vinegar and dish soap, tried two different soaps, and even added sugar and the fruit flies ignored the home made version of this trap.But the honey and liquid lure on top snagged them.

title: Gets the vast majority with ease
body: I can confidently say that this trap does not disappoint. From the moment I set it up, I noticed a dramatic reduction in the number of fruit flies that were previously hovering around my fruit bowl and trash can.The trap's design allows you to actually see the trapped fruit flies floating, which for me, is a clear indicator that it's doing its job effectively. Every time I glanced over at the trap, it gave me the satisfaction of knowing that fewer of these little nuisances were flying around. Within a few days, I only spotted one or two fruit flies occasionally, which is a vast improvement from the multitude that once took residence in my home.However, it's worth noting that while it has significantly reduced the fruit fly population, it hasn't eradicated every single one. But given that I haven't ventured much into other fruit fly control products, this one certainly stands out in terms of effectiveness.In conclusion, if you're looking for a solution that will majorly cut down your fruit fly issue, this trap is a worthy contender. It might not get every single fly, but it certainly puts a substantial dent in their numbers. Highly recommended for anyone facing a similar nuisance!

I read a bunch of reviews and ordered these Terro fruit fly traps and another product that contained a blue light with sticky paper. After one week the fruit fly problem was resolved. The Terro trap was full of fruit flys and the other was not. I attached photos of the results. No more fruit flys in the kitchen - amazing result! I did learn a couple of things. I had set up all 4 traps and noticed that one trap got all the action even though it was not near the counter where the flys were most active. I realized that the active trap was the one that I had handled the least, and it wasn’t near the counter that I sprayed and cleaned daily. I think the counter cleaner mist must have gotten on the other devices reducing their efficiency.

title: Fruit Flies Stole My Children, But They Were Only The Soldiers
body: If you are looking to murder fruit flies with little effort, this is the tool.Fruit flies lied to me, stole my children, hacked into my bank account to deplete me of resources and bribed a senator to invalidate my citizenship in my home country. When I set out for revenge, I knew there were many fruit flies in the world, but I had no idea how many. They are an uncountable force, and they are taking every opportunity to invade your home.When I set out the magic apple it finally allowed me to capture and interrogate a fruit fly I had in a headlock about the whereabouts of my children, and by the the time this occurred there were already 10-20-30, nay, legion of fruit flies that were already deposed.If you are living a life on the edge, seeking your family and have discovered that fruit flies are responsible, then there is no other option. Just know: fruit flies work for other bugs, and those bugs do not have the same weaknesses, and do not respond the same to interrogation. While a headlock worked on fruit flies to gather leads about my children, these larger bugs may require a Brazilian Jiu Jitsu triangle to submit.Again, if you are like me and protecting or searching for your children, you must use the most extreme option, and this is what has helped me in my journey so far.

title: Works really well to get rid of a full scale fruit fly invasion!
body: Works really well to get rid of a full scale fruit fly invasion!I really hate fruit flies, and they can be very hard to get rid of if you get a big invasion.  I had a mishap where a banana fell behind something and I didn't realize it until a fruit fly population exploded out of that overripe banana.  I had one of those glue board traps where a UV light and a fan suck the flies in, but that only captured so many per day, not enough to quell the invasion.  I was overrun with them because they weren't all getting captured and were multiplying like mad.So I tried this chemical trap, and it immediately attracted them and started getting rid of hordes of them every day.  My only suggestion is that the liquid used has a weird vinegary and sweetish smell like rotting fruit, but I guess that's the whole point to attract them since they're fruit eaters.  I put it in a corner away from where I am so I don't smell it, but the flies go straight for it.I've given up bananas until I'm sure these nasty little critters are long gone.  They hijack in on bananas, so from now on if I see even one fly, this trap is coming out again!

title: Works on Phorid Flies (with Modification)
body: If the flies are mostly crawling on the top but rarely going into the trap, there's a good chance they're phorid flies, not fruit flies. They look very similar, but phorid flies are more attracted to water and decay than fruit flies. As recommended by some other reviewers, I added yellow sticky flowers to the top of the trap (see photo) and that has worked much better for me than the apples alone.My infestation is not fully gone yet, but there are definitely fewer flies than when I was using UV traps only. I have heard that powdered liver is a better attractant than vinegar for phorid flies, so I'm going to experiment with that next.

body: Purchased to trap phorid/scuttle flies, and worked great. We captured dozens of flies. title: WORKS AMAZING
body: These work amazingly well. I made a mistake and went camping for the weekend. I usually remember to remove perishables. But this time we forgot a couple bananas in the fruitbowl. This was just enough to spark a problem. I bought 3 different types of fly and fruit fly traps and baits to see which works the best.These arrived first so I set them out. They got little interest the first day. But by day 2 they were very interested in them. Day 3 they are all over these traps and you can literally watch them climbing in to their demise.I couldn't be more pleased. They seem to not have a way back out once they go in. I can already see some floating in the liquid bait. So all in all I would say success. I was trying home made remedies in the past with mason jars and plastic wrap. But I have to say this is far less hassle and messes. Takes up less space. And I will definitely be stocking more of this bait liquid.I had bought some cute little ones that look like succulent plants. And they aren't getting the same attention. So I am going to try to wash one out and refill with the bait from these terro ones. As I am thinking it might be the mixture of this terro bait.I hope so Because I far prefer the aesthetic of these plant type ones. The little red apples or tomatoes are pretty basic almost eye catching. And if you are trying to knock out a fruit fly problem you don't exactly want a giant red eyesore in your kitchen drawing attention to your little problem. I really wish these were less tacky looking. The bright red color would definitely attract small fingers. As they look like a small ball or toy.
date: Reviewed in the United States on October 10, 2024
author: Mamabear C.

title: Total elimination 🫶
body: I’m not sure what’s going on with 2025 but it’s been rough! Up to an including the renegade gnats that seemed to have all decided to live well into December. 😡 I thought I could wait them out but they held on!!! I tried the plug in things and they werent effective in getting rid of them all. Then I bought this product. I placed all four and Within 24 hours all those suckers were NOWHERE to be found. Only one downside is the vinegar smell but whatever puts an end to that annoyance is a fair trade off
date: Reviewed in the United States on December 4, 2025
author: A. Beal

title: Like Hotel California for fruit flies!
body: I'm totally amazed at how effective the Terro Fruit Fly Trap is. My kitchen was, for a lack of words, infested. No matter what I did, the little buggers returned in numbers. The one thing I found was you have to have a little patience -  it doesn't take long. Set up is easy and not messy. Depending on how bad your problem, you will notice their curiosity to start off slow. Don't fret, give the little apple shaped container to do its thing. After a day or so, more and more buggers came to visit. They checked in but not out - almost like Hotel California for fruit flies. As of right now, I can honestly say my problem has been resolved. Totally worth the money - totally effective. Highly recommended.
date: Reviewed in the United States on October 30, 2025
